chosen_title: Mumbai IT professional claims ₹3.91 lakh TDS, gets ₹3.36 lakh tax demand as employer failed to deposit — ITAT Mumbai rules for employee
caption:
Your TDS was cut every month. Your payslip proved it. Then came a ₹3.36 lakh tax demand.

A Mumbai IT professional had ₹3.91 lakh deducted as TDS — but her employer never deposited it with the government. When she filed her ITR claiming the full credit, the department raised a demand because Form 26AS reflected only a fraction. ITAT Mumbai ruled in her favour: an employee cannot be penalised for the employer's failure to deposit TDS, and full credit must be given based on Form 16.

The practical check: say your Form 16 shows ₹80,000 TDS but Form 26AS reflects only ₹25,000 — that ₹55,000 gap alone can trigger a demand notice. Illustration only; actual numbers vary. Match both every quarter, not just at filing time. If there's a gap, flag it to HR in writing immediately and keep records.

What to do: before you file your ITR (due July 31), download Form 26AS and AIS at incometax.gov.in and verify every TDS entry. Any mismatch — get professional help fast, not after the notice lands.

Got a demand notice or a Form 26AS mismatch? WhatsApp us — we'll sort it.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: ITAT Mumbai, as reported on taxguru.in (June 2026)

slides.label: TAX FILES
slides.image_prompt: A crumpled government income-tax demand notice resting on a corporate office desk beside a neatly printed salary payslip, dramatic blue-grey moody lighting with a single pool of desk-lamp light, cinematic close-up, no readable text or numbers visible anywhere in frame
slides.headline: Her employer cut ₹3.91 lakh in TDS. Then the govt sent her a ₹3.36 lakh demand.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Tax demand issued to a Mumbai IT professional — for TDS her employer deducted from her salary every month but quietly never deposited with the government.

slides.label: TAX FILES
slides.headline: The govt never got the money. But they came after her.

slides.label: MYTH vs FACT
slides.myth: If TDS appears on your payslip, it's been deposited — you're fully covered at filing time.
slides.truth: Not always. If your employer doesn't deposit it, the credit won't show in Form 26AS, and the Income Tax Department can raise a demand directly on you.

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.title: Check this before July 31 →
points: Match every TDS entry against your monthly payslips
Gap between Form 16 and 26AS? Email HR — in writing, keep the thread
Never file ITR ignoring a mismatch and hope for the best

slides.headline: ITAT ruled: employer's fault, not yours.
slides.caption: Fight back with the right paperwork — Form 16 is your proof.
